subcategories.name: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers
subcategories.description: Appointments, promotions, confirmation, and transfers under PSR.

questions.question: Who has the authority to make appointments to posts graded GL.07–**GL.17** in the Federal Public Service?
questions.explanation: PSR 020102 states that the Federal Civil Service Commission (FCSC) makes appointments to posts graded GL.07–GL.17. (PSR 020102).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognized category of appointment under the **PSR**'?
questions.explanation: PSR 020202 lists trainees, probationable pensionable appointments, non-pensionable contract, acting and special appointments. 'Permanent appointment without formality' is not a PSR category. (PSR 020202).

questions.question: What minimum percentage of all recruitments is reserved for persons with disability?
questions.explanation: PSR 020202(f) mandates that 5% of all recruitments in the Service be reserved for persons with disability. (PSR 020202).

questions.question: What is the standard probationary period for officers appointed on probation to pensionable establishment?
questions.explanation: PSR 020301 provides that officers on probation serve for two years before confirmation, subject to reductions in specific circumstances. (PSR 020301).

questions.question: During probation an officer must pass which of the following to be eligible for confirmation
options: The prescribed examination appropriate to the appointment.
A written testimonial from previous employer only.
No requirement to pass any examination.
questions.explanation: PSR 020302 requires that within probation an officer pass the prescribed examination appropriate to the appointment. (PSR 020302).

questions.question: Who is authorized, subject to delegation limits, to appoint eligible candidates to posts **GL.06** and below?
options: Head of the Civil Service directly.
questions.explanation: PSR 020101 states Permanent Secretaries/Heads of Extra-Ministerial Offices may appoint to posts where powers have been delegated — typically GL.06 and below. (PSR 020101).

questions.question: Which procedure is required for appointment to senior posts (**GL.07** and above)?
options: Appointment by Federal Civil Service Commission after advertisement.
Automatic promotion based on years of service.
Selection by departmental head without advertisement.
questions.explanation: PSR 020102 explains that FCSC appoints to GL.07–17 and such appointments are made into available vacancies after advertisement. (PSR 020102).

questions.question: Which form must applicants for senior posts complete as part of the **FCSC** process?
questions.explanation: PSR 020208(a) requires applicants for senior posts to complete the Federal Civil Service Commission Application Form (FC.2). (PSR 020208).

questions.question: When does the incremental date of an officer appointed or promoted under the PSR become effective?
options: 1 April each year under standard practice.
1 January each year after six months on grade with good performance.
Date of appointment in every case.
questions.explanation: Under the PSR, the incremental date becomes effective on 1 January each year once the officer has spent at least six months on the grade with satisfactory performance.

questions.question: Which of the following must be submitted to the Office of the Head of the Civil Service, OAGF and PenCom within one month of appointment?
questions.explanation: PSR 020107 requires staff record form (Gen. 60) to be sent to OHCSF, OAGF, Auditor-General and PenCom within one month of appointment. (PSR 020107).

questions.question: Under **PSR**, membership of which type of organization by a public officer is prohibited
questions.explanation: PSR 020211 prohibits public officers from being members of secret societies; contravention is serious misconduct. (PSR 020211).

questions.question: Under what condition may the normal two-year probation be reduced to not less than six months?
options: Reduction by counting prior satisfactory public service in cognate posts.
Reduction on the officer's personal request alone.
Reduction on the ground of the officer being under thirty years of age.
questions.explanation: The probationary period may be reduced by counting prior satisfactory public service in cognate posts, but it cannot be reduced below six months.

questions.question: If an officer's probation is extended by the **FCSC**, what incremental consequence may the Commission impose
questions.explanation: PSR 020301 states extensions may result in an incremental penalty as referred in Rule 040206. (PSR 020301; PSR 040206).

questions.question: Which examinations are officers in senior posts required to pass while on probation?
options: COMPRO I and COMPRO II as prescribed for the relevant level.
questions.explanation: Senior officers on probation are required to pass the prescribed confirmation examinations for their level, including the appropriate COMPRO requirement where applicable.

questions.question: Which committee handles recruitment/selection for Junior Staff (**GL.06** and below) in a Ministry?
questions.explanation: PSR 020103 states each Ministry shall appoint Junior Staff on GL.06 and below handled by the Junior Staff Committee. (PSR 020103).

questions.question: Which condition applies to transfer or secondment to another service under the PSR?
options: Officer must be confirmed and suitably qualified under the Scheme of Service.
Officer must be less than thirty years old.
Officer may move without prior approval.
questions.explanation: Transfer or secondment requires a confirmed officer whose qualifications fit the applicable Scheme of Service and the approval process laid down by the PSR.

questions.question: How long is an initial secondment to the service of another government, approved body or international organization allowed in the first instance?
questions.explanation: PSR 020505(f) states secondment to another government/approved body/international organization shall be for a maximum of two years in the first instance. (PSR 020505).

questions.question: If a secondment is in the public interest, what is the maximum total period allowed under **PSR**'
questions.explanation: PSR 020505(g) provides that if secondment is in public interest the period shall not exceed six years. (PSR 020505).

questions.question: Which body must approve transfers/secondments of senior officers to parastatals under the supervision of a Ministry?
options: The Federal Civil Service Commission / Board.
questions.explanation: PSR 020507(ii) mandates approval of the Federal Civil Service Commission/Board before transferring/seconding senior officers to parastatals under a Ministry. (PSR 020507).

questions.question: What periodic return must MDAs make concerning staff on transfer or secondment?
options: Quarterly return to the FCSC and OHCSF on staff on transfer or secondment.
questions.explanation: MDAs are required to make quarterly returns to the Federal Civil Service Commission and the Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation on staff on transfer or secondment.

questions.question: Which PSR rule sets out posting as the initial assignment or reassignment of an officer to a job schedule?
questions.explanation: PSR 020601 sets out posting as the initial assignment or reassignment of an officer to a position or job schedule. The item therefore tests identification of the correct rule reference.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the according to **psr**, posting of officers to posts outside their professional cadre is?
questions.explanation: PSR 020602(ii) clearly states posting of officers to posts outside their professional cadres is prohibited. (PSR 020602).

questions.question: Under the **PSR**, after how many years must pool officers in the Service be mandatorily posted?
questions.explanation: PSR 020602(iii) makes the posting of pool officers mandatory after every four years.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the failure of an officer to proceed on posting is regarded by **psr** as?
questions.explanation: PSR 020602(iv) states failure to proceed on posting is regarded as misconduct. (PSR 020602).

questions.question: How are acting appointments treated under the PSR?
options: A guaranteed path to substantive promotion.
An interim administrative arrangement rather than a promotion trial.
A post with a fixed minimum duration of two years.
An arrangement restricted to junior staff only.
questions.explanation: The PSR treats acting appointments as interim arrangements made for administrative necessity, not as a trial run for substantive promotion.

questions.question: Which of the following is required before an officer can transfer-on-promotion to another **MDA** according to **PSR**'?
options: Guarantee by the receiving Permanent Secretary.
questions.explanation: PSR 020506 states no applicant shall be considered for transfer-on-promotion except in response to an advertised post. (PSR 020506).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule requires that officers complete Personal Particulars Form Gen.60 immediately upon assuming duty?
questions.explanation: PSR 020209 (Personal Particulars) requires newly appointed officers to enter personal particulars on Form Gen.60 immediately upon assuming duty. (PSR 020209).

questions.question: Which rule directs that confirmation recommendations for officers holding senior posts should be made in the progress report prescribed in Chapter 5?
questions.explanation: PSR 020304 specifies that recommendations regarding confirmation of officers holding senior posts shall be made in the progress report prescribed in Chapter 5. (PSR 020304).

questions.question: Can a confirmed officer advanced to a training grade be regarded as having been seconded under the PSR?
options: Absence of secondment effect on the ground of training-grade status.
Secondment treatment for the duration of the training period.
Secondment treatment only with written consent.
Secondment treatment only for foreign training.
questions.explanation: A confirmed officer advanced to a training grade may be treated as seconded for the duration of the approved training arrangement.

questions.question: Which rule prescribes that appointment to the National Youth Service Corps Presidential Merit Award winners is automatic regardless of vacancy?
questions.explanation: PSR 020202(i) (subparagraph in PSR 020202) states Ministries shall accept appointments made by FCSC to Presidential Merit Award winners whose employment is automatic regardless of vacancy constraints. (PSR 020202).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule mandates that advertisement for **FCSC** vacancies appear in three national newspapers and the Commission's website with a six-week application deadline?
questions.explanation: PSR 020102(i) provides that FCSC advertisements for GL.07-17 vacancies be in three national newspapers and the Commission's website with a six-week deadline. (PSR 020102).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule states that an officer must serve a minimum of six months in his original post before applying for transfer to another senior post?
questions.explanation: PSR 020307 specifies that an officer must have served a minimum of six months in his original post before seeking transfer to another post. (PSR 020307).

questions.question: For a transfer application under **PSR** 020307, which of the following must be included in the submission to **FCSC**'
options: Statement of applicant's performance, suitability and recommendation.
questions.explanation: PSR 020307 requires the Permanent Secretary to forward application with statement on how applicant performed, suitability and recommendation. (PSR 020307).

questions.question: Which rule prohibits an **MDA** from accepting staff on secondment without endorsement by the Federal Civil Service Commission or Board?
questions.explanation: PSR 020507(iii) prohibits an MDA from accepting staff on secondment without endorsement by the Federal Civil Service Commission or Board.

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule mandates that a newly recruited officer who has spent six months and above shall be allowed to sit for compulsory examinations for confirmation?
questions.explanation: PSR 020306 permits newly recruited officers who have served six months and above to sit for compulsory confirmation examinations. (PSR 020306).

questions.question: Which Chapter contains detailed syllabuses and prescribed confirmation examinations (COMPRO) for senior executive and professional officers?
questions.explanation: Chapter 3 of the PSR contains prescribed examinations (COMPRO) and syllabuses for senior executive and professional officers. (Chapter 3; PSR 0302xx).

questions.question: Which statement is true about contract appointments under the PSR?
options: Automatic pension entitlement in every case.
Temporary and non-pensionable appointment recorded in a formal agreement.
Automatic conversion to permanent status after two years.
questions.explanation: Contract appointments are temporary, non-pensionable arrangements and must be governed by formal written terms.

questions.question: Which of the following circumstances may warrant a contract appointment under **PSR**'?
options: When a post can be filled internally immediately.
Where service lacks officers with required specialized skills.
questions.explanation: PSR 020403(i) permits contract appointments if the Service lacks officers with the required specialized skills as provided in approved Schemes of Service. (PSR 020403).

questions.question: Who is responsible for appraising an officer seconded to another MDA under the PSR?
options: Appraisal by the releasing agency only.
Appraisal by the Federal Civil Service Commission only.
questions.explanation: An officer on secondment is appraised by the receiving MDA because that organization supervises the officer's actual work during the secondment period.

questions.question: Which rule explains that staff sent on secondment shall continue to be treated as holding their substantive post and paid by the benefiting organization while emoluments may be the responsibility of releasing **MDA** if differential exists?
questions.explanation: PSR 020505(h) & related paragraphs explain continuing substantive status and the allocation of payment responsibilities where salary differentials exist. (PSR 020505).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule disqualifies appointment without prior specific approval where a candidate has been convicted of a criminal offence or dismissed from public service?
questions.explanation: PSR 020207(a) requires prior specific approval of FCSC or Board where candidate was convicted or previously dismissed from public service. (PSR 020207).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule indicates that the date of birth recorded on appointment shall not be changed throughout an officer’s career?
questions.explanation: PSR 020107(ii) prescribes the date of birth recorded on appointment shall not be changed and change is serious misconduct. (PSR 020107).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule governs the withholding, deferring and restoration of increments (relates to incremental penalties)?
questions.explanation: PSR Chapter 4 (Rules 040206–040209) explains withholding/deferring increments and restoration, referenced in incremental penalties for probation extensions. (PSR 040206–040209).

questions.question: Under **PSR**, which body must be included as observers in Junior Staff and Senior Staff Committees in parastatals
options: Representative of Department of Human Resource Management of supervising Ministry and an observer from OHCSF.
Procedurally, only the board chairman within applicable policy conditions.
In practice, only the chief executive officer in authorized administrative workflow.
Under formal rules, no observers required under established public service standards.
questions.explanation: PSR 020305 & 170306 indicate JSC/SSC in parastatals shall include rep of HRM of supervising Ministry and an observer from OHCSF. (PSR 020305; PSR 170306).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule sets the advertisement and selection linkage between **FCSC** and **OHCSF** for entry at GL.07–10?
options: PSR 020102(iv) combined with selection modalities prescribed by FCSC.
Procedurally, psr 020103 only within applicable policy conditions.
In practice, psr 020204 in authorized administrative workflow.
Under formal rules, psr 020701 under established public service standards.
questions.explanation: PSR 020102(iv) and its subsections prescribe annual competitive entry exam for GL.07–10 to be conducted by FCSC with OHCSF and training institutes. (PSR 020102).

questions.question: Who may reduce the probationary period to less than two years, but not less than six months, by counting prior satisfactory public service?
options: Reduction by the Permanent Secretary acting alone in every case.
Reduction by the Head of Service acting without reference to appointing authority.
Reduction by the appointing authority subject to PSR conditions.
No reduction on the ground of fixed probation in all cases.
questions.explanation: The appointing authority may reduce probation by counting prior satisfactory public service, provided the reduction stays within PSR conditions.

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule governs that transfer from one senior post to another requires prior approval of the **FCSC** or appropriate board?
questions.explanation: PSR 020307 states transfer from one senior post to another requires prior approval of the FCSC or appropriate Board. (PSR 020307).

questions.question: Which **PSR** provision states that appointment documents and staff records must be typed/filled in ink and one copy sent to the National Record Centre and other offices?
questions.explanation: PSR 020107 requires Staff Record Form (Gen.60) to be completed in ink and copies sent to National Record Centre, OAGF, AGF and PenCom. (PSR 020107).

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule requires that in implementing postings, fairness must be ensured so posting is not used as a tool for coercion, punishment or cronyism?
questions.explanation: PSR 020603 states fairness is an underlying principle in postings to ensure the system is not used for coercion, punishment or cronyism. (PSR 020603).

questions.question: In the context of Appointments, Promotions & Transfers, which action best demonstrates sound governance?
options: Use of approved procedures with complete records.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Sound governance in appointments, promotions, and transfers depends on approved procedures, complete records, and decisions that can be reviewed later.

questions.question: For effective appointments, promotions & transfers, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure appointments, promotions & transfers compliance
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on escalation of material compliance gaps within Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Early identification of control gaps with prompt escalation.
questions.explanation: Escalation of material compliance gaps is strongest when control weaknesses are identified early and referred promptly for higher review.

questions.question: When handling appointments, promotions & transfers, which choice reflects proper appointment governance standards?
options: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain disciplinary process in Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Observe due process, fair hearing, and documented decisions.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Observe due process, fair hearing, and documented decisions. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Appointments, Promotions & Transfers, which action best demonstrates leave administration
options: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: For effective Appointments, Promotions & Transfers administration, which approach best preserves promotion standards?
options: Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Promotion standards are protected when eligibility is confirmed before any advancement recommendation is made.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on circular compliance within Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Align practice with current circular directives and PSR rules.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Align practice with current circular directives and psr rules. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers.

questions.question: When handling Appointments, Promotions & Transfers matters, which choice best reflects proper documented procedure?
options: Use of documented procedure with complete recordkeeping.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Proper documented procedure means following the approved steps and keeping complete records that support later review.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain compliance assurance in Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Appointments, Promotions & Transfers, which action best demonstrates public accountability?
questions.explanation: Public accountability is strongest when decisions are traceable and supported by evidence-based reasons.

questions.question: For effective appointments, promotions & transfers, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure service integrity
options: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on risk control within Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Early risk identification with documented mitigation.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Risk control improves when risks are identified early, mitigation is documented, and corrective action is tracked.

questions.question: When handling appointments, promotions & transfers, which choice reflects proper decision transparency standards?
options: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain operational discipline in Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Approved workflow use with output verification.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Operational discipline is sustained when approved workflows are followed and outputs are checked before closure.

questions.question: In the context of Appointments, Promotions & Transfers, which action best demonstrates citizen-focused service
options: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: For effective Appointments, Promotions & Transfers administration, which approach best preserves record management?
options: Accurate file maintenance with status updates.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Record management is preserved by accurate file maintenance and timely status updates at each control point.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on performance standards within Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: When handling Appointments, Promotions & Transfers matters, which choice best reflects sound governance standards?
options: Use of approved procedures with complete records.
questions.explanation: Sound governance standards require approved procedures, complete records, and decisions that can be reviewed objectively.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain appointments, promotions & transfers compliance in Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Appointments, Promotions & Transfers, which action best demonstrates appointments, promotions & transfers risk management
options: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: For effective appointments, promotions & transfers, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure appointment governance
options: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on disciplinary process within Appointments, Promotions & Transfers?
options: Due process, fair hearing, and documented decisions.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: A sound disciplinary process depends on due process, fair hearing, and documented decisions that can withstand review.

questions.question: When handling appointments, promotions & transfers, which choice reflects proper leave administration standards?
options: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in appointments, promotions & transfers Reference: Appointments, Promotions & Transfers - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain promotion standards in Appointments, Promotions & Transfers administration?
options: Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Promotion standards remain credible when eligibility is confirmed before advancement is recommended.

questions.question: A unit handling Appointments, Promotions & Transfers receives a case with competing priorities. Which action best preserves compliance and service quality?
options: Consistent application of PSR provisions with auditable records.
Bypassed review checkpoints under time pressure.
questions.explanation: Compliance and service quality are preserved when the applicable PSR provisions are applied consistently and the record remains auditable.

questions.question: Who has the official authority to approve the appointment of Junior Staff?
options: The Permanent Secretary/Head of Extra-Ministerial Offices.
questions.explanation: Junior staff appointments are handled through the appropriate committee structure, so the officer or committee with delegated appointments authority is the correct focus of this item.

questions.question: Can the date of birth recorded on appointment be changed?
options: No, date of birth recorded on appointment shall not be changed throughout the career.
questions.explanation: The PSR treats the date of birth recorded at appointment as a formal personnel record, so it is not casually changed after appointment.

questions.question: What is the official definition of "Recruitment" according to the relevant regulations?
options: Filling of vacancies by direct appointment of persons not already in any Public Service of Nigeria.
questions.explanation: Recruitment means the process of bringing suitable persons into the public service to fill authorized posts.

questions.question: Which of the following is a category of appointment to the Federal Public Service?
questions.explanation: A probationary pensionable appointment is a recognized appointment category under the PSR, so the question asks you to identify that class correctly.

questions.question: What type of appointment may be given to a candidate who requires additional experience?
questions.explanation: A trainee or pupil appointment is used when a candidate needs additional experience before full deployment, so the correct option is the one describing that arrangement.

questions.question: What happens after the completion of the "trainee period"?
options: The pupil may be appointed to the full grade on probation.
questions.explanation: After the trainee period, the officer normally moves on to the next applicable appointment status or confirmation step under the PSR.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the all first appointments to pensionable posts other than trainee posts will be?
questions.explanation: First appointments to pensionable posts are normally probationary rather than immediately permanent, which is why the probation-related option is correct.

questions.question: Is an Officer confirmed in a lower post regarded as on probation in a higher post to which he is converted?
questions.explanation: An officer confirmed in a lower post is not automatically on probation in a higher post merely because of conversion, so the PSR distinction matters here.

questions.question: Where are the requisite qualifications for appointment provided?
questions.explanation: The requisite qualifications for appointment are set out in the approved Scheme of Service, which is why that source is the correct answer.

questions.question: In which case is prior specific approval required for an appointment?
options: If candidate has been convicted of a criminal offence.
questions.explanation: Prior specific approval is required where the PSR rules expressly reserve appointment authority or require clearance before the post can be filled.

questions.question: How long are officers on probation required to serve?
questions.explanation: Officers on probation are required to serve the prescribed probationary period before confirmation unless the PSR allows a reduction in approved cases.

questions.question: Under what condition may probation be reduced?
options: Deduction of previous satisfactory Public Service in cognate status.
questions.explanation: Correct option: B (Deduction of previous satisfactory Public Service in cognate status.).

questions.question: What may result from an extension of probation?
questions.explanation: An extension of probation can attract a penalty on increment, so the item tests the consequence tied to unsatisfactory progress.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines the term "an Officer required to do during probation" according to official standards?
options: Pass the prescribed examination appropriate to appointment.
questions.explanation: During probation, an officer must continue to show satisfactory conduct, performance, and compliance with the PSR so confirmation can be considered.

questions.question: For senior posts, what do prescribed examinations include?
options: Those described in Chapter 7 and individual offer of appointment.
questions.explanation: Correct option: B (Those described in Chapter 7 and individual offer of appointment.).

questions.question: What happens if a clerical Officer fails the exam during probation?
questions.explanation: If a clerical officer fails the prescribed examination during probation, the PSR treats that as a failure to satisfy the condition for confirmation.

questions.question: What happens if a clerical Officer fails the exam at the end of an extended probation?
questions.explanation: Failure of the clerical officer at the end of an extended probation means confirmation cannot follow, which is why the adverse outcome option is correct.

questions.question: For professional cadres, what is the condition for confirmation?
options: Satisfactory completion of probation and possession of Scheme of Service qualifications.
questions.explanation: Correct option: B (Satisfactory completion of probation and possession of Scheme of Service qualifications.).

questions.question: When is an officer eligible for confirmation?
options: Eligibility after satisfactory probation and favourable assessment.
Eligibility after every examination regardless of report.
Eligibility after a ceremonial confirmation exercise.
questions.explanation: An officer becomes eligible for confirmation after satisfactory completion of probation together with a favourable assessment from the appropriate authority.

questions.question: Who conveys the confirmation to the Officer?
options: FCSC or Permanent Secretary as the case may be.
questions.explanation: The confirmation result is conveyed through the proper supervisory or administrative channel so the officer receives formal notice of the decision.

questions.question: What happens if service during probation is unsatisfactory?
questions.explanation: Unsatisfactory service during probation can prevent confirmation, because the PSR requires both conduct and performance to be acceptable.

questions.question: Who can approve termination of an Officer on probation?
options: FCSC or Permanent Secretary as the case may be.
questions.explanation: Termination of an officer on probation must be approved by the authorized appointing or disciplinary authority, not by an individual officer acting alone.

questions.question: What must an Officer possess to be eligible for promotion?
options: Satisfactory APERs for the three preceding years.
questions.explanation: Promotion eligibility depends on meeting the PSR conditions for service length, performance, and any required examinations or assessments.

questions.question: Who conducts promotion examinations for GL. 07 and above?
questions.explanation: Promotion examinations for GL.07 and above are conducted under the approved service processes for senior officers, which is why the designated PSR body is correct.

questions.question: When does a promotion take effect?
options: Date specified in the promotion letter.
questions.explanation: A promotion normally takes effect from the date prescribed in the PSR or the date approved in the promotion process, not from an arbitrary later date.

questions.question: Can an Officer refuse a promotion?
options: Yes, but they may not be considered for further promotion for a specified period.
questions.explanation: An officer cannot simply refuse a duly approved promotion without consequence, because promotion is part of the service structure and may carry obligations.

questions.question: What happens if an Officer refuses a promotion?
options: Not considered for further promotion for two (2) years except on application to FCSC.
questions.explanation: If an officer refuses promotion, the PSR allows the service to treat that refusal as a matter requiring administrative consequences.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the term "acting appointment" as used in the Public Service Rules?
options: Temporary assignment to perform duties of a higher post.
questions.explanation: An acting appointment is a temporary arrangement made to fill a post pending substantive appointment or confirmation.

questions.question: What happens if acting appointment exceeds six months without approval?
questions.explanation: Acting beyond the approved period without approval is not permitted, so the PSR consequence is the key point being tested here.

questions.question: Who has the official authority to approve the transfer of an Officer?
questions.explanation: Transfers require approval by the proper authority, which is why the question focuses on who can lawfully approve the transfer.

questions.question: What happens to seniority upon transfer?
options: Seniority in former Ministry shall be maintained in the new Ministry.
questions.explanation: Transfer affects seniority according to the PSR rules governing the receiving and losing cadres, so the correct option is the one that reflects that rule.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the term "secondment" as used in the Public Service Rules?
options: Temporary transfer of an Officer to another Service or Organisation.
questions.explanation: Secondment is a temporary placement of an officer in another post or organization while the original appointment remains in force.

questions.question: What happens at the end of secondment?
options: Officer normally reverts to former post.
questions.explanation: At the end of secondment, the officer normally returns to the parent service or office unless the PSR or approval conditions say otherwise.

questions.question: Under what circumstance can appointment be terminated?
options: Poor performance alone in every case.
Termination considered to be in the public interest.
Termination only with the officer's consent.
Termination only after a disciplinary conviction.
questions.explanation: The PSR permits termination of appointment when the appointing authority determines that it is in the public interest.

questions.question: What is the consequence for an officer who changes their date of birth recorded upon appointment throughout their career?
questions.explanation: Date of birth recorded on appointment by an officer shall not be changed throughout the career of the Officer. Any contravention shall be regarded as an act of serious misconduct.

questions.question: Which officer category is handled by the Junior Staff Committee (JSC) of each Ministry/Office regarding appointments?
options: Junior Staff on GL. 06 and below.
questions.explanation: Each Ministry/Extra-Ministerial Office shall appoint Junior Staff on GL. 06 and below. This shall be handled by the Junior Staff Committee.

subcategories.name: Discipline & Misconduct
subcategories.description: Misconduct categories, disciplinary process, and sanctions.

questions.question: Which of the following is classified as serious misconduct under the **PSR**'?
options: Failure to submit reports on time.
questions.explanation: According to PSR 100201, embezzlement of public funds constitutes serious misconduct and may lead to dismissal from service.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the under **psr** 2021, misconduct is defined as?
options: Any minor offence committed by a public officer.
Any act of wrongdoing or improper behaviour inconsistent with the public service code.
An act punishable by criminal law only.
An offence that affects only senior officers.
questions.explanation: PSR 100101 defines misconduct as a specific act of wrongdoing or improper behaviour inconsistent with the Public Service Code of Conduct and Ethics.

questions.question: Which of the following may amount to misconduct as per **PSR**'?
questions.explanation: PSR 100202 lists neglect of duty as an example of misconduct for which disciplinary action may be taken.

questions.question: Serious misconduct shall lead to which disciplinary outcome
questions.explanation: PSR 100201 provides that serious misconduct is a grave offence punishable by dismissal.

questions.question: Before any disciplinary action is taken against an officer, what must first be issued
questions.explanation: PSR 100302 states that a query must be issued to an officer alleged to have committed misconduct before disciplinary action is taken.

questions.question: What is the time limit for an officer to reply a query under **PSR**'?
options: 72 hours or as may be specified.
questions.explanation: PSR 100302 requires an officer to reply a query within 72 hours or within a specified time in the query letter.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an example of serious misconduct?
options: Failure to submit annual leave application.
questions.explanation: PSR 100201 lists dishonesty, falsification of records, and insubordination as serious misconduct, but failure to submit leave application is not classified as such.

questions.question: What is the first step in disciplinary proceedings for misconduct?
questions.explanation: According to PSR 100302, disciplinary proceedings begin with issuance of a query outlining the allegation to the officer.

questions.question: Who is responsible for initiating disciplinary action against a public officer?
options: Initiation by the Head of Service in ordinary cases.
Initiation by the Permanent Secretary or Head of Extra-Ministerial Office.
Initiation by the President in routine disciplinary cases.
Initiation by the Auditor-General in ordinary service matters.
questions.explanation: PSR 100303 assigns responsibility for initiating disciplinary action to the Permanent Secretary or Head of Extra-Ministerial Office.

questions.question: When an officer is under investigation for serious misconduct, what action may be taken to prevent interference?
questions.explanation: PSR 100305 allows for interdiction of an officer facing serious misconduct charges to prevent interference during investigation.

questions.question: During interdiction, what portion of an officer’s salary is paid
questions.explanation: PSR 100305 stipulates that an officer on interdiction shall be entitled to half of his salary pending the conclusion of investigation.

questions.question: Suspension of an officer is used when what condition exists?
options: Investigation of a serious case involving public interest.
questions.explanation: PSR 100306 provides that suspension may be used where a serious case involving public interest is under investigation.

questions.question: For a case of misconduct, which committee recommends penalties to the Federal Civil Service Commission
questions.explanation: PSR 100304 specifies that the Senior Staff Committee (Disciplinary) considers disciplinary cases and makes recommendations to the FCSC.

questions.question: When an officer is found guilty of misconduct but not dismissed, which punishment may be applied?
questions.explanation: PSR 100310 lists punishments such as reduction in rank, withholding increment, or written warning when misconduct does not warrant dismissal.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the an officer dismissed for serious misconduct loses all claims to?
options: Salary and benefits from date of dismissal.
questions.explanation: PSR 100311 states that a dismissed officer forfeits all claims to remuneration and terminal benefits as from the date of dismissal.

questions.question: Where a disciplinary case has been pending for over six months without conclusion, the responsible authority must?
options: Submit a progress report to the Head of Service.
questions.explanation: PSR 100309 requires that where a case remains pending beyond six months, a progress report must be submitted to the Head of Service.

questions.question: What punishment applies to an officer convicted by a court for a criminal offence involving dishonesty?
questions.explanation: PSR 100312 provides that an officer convicted of a criminal offence involving dishonesty shall be dismissed from service.

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule provides that disciplinary action shall not commence unless the officer has been informed in writing of the charges?
questions.explanation: PSR 100302 mandates written communication of allegations before disciplinary action begins.

questions.question: In cases involving fraud, falsification, or dishonesty, which agency must be notified?
options: Notification to the Federal Character Commission.
Notification to the Office of the Auditor-General.
Notification to the Independent Corrupt Practices and Other Related Offences Commission (ICPC).
Notification to the Bureau of Public Service Reforms.
questions.explanation: PSR 100307 directs ministries to notify ICPC or another appropriate anti-corruption agency when a case involves fraud, falsification, or dishonesty.

questions.question: When is an officer deemed to have abandoned duty under **PSR**'?
options: After 10 consecutive working days without permission.
questions.explanation: PSR 100308 considers an officer absent without leave for 10 working days as having abandoned duty and liable to dismissal.

questions.question: What happens when an officer under interdiction is found not guilty of the offence?
options: Continuation of interdiction for record purposes.
Reinstatement with refund of withheld salary.
questions.explanation: PSR 100305 states that when an interdicted officer is exonerated, the officer is reinstated and the balance of salary withheld during interdiction is refunded.

questions.question: Under **PSR** 2021, which action may be taken against an officer found guilty of misconduct but whose offence is not grave enough for dismissal
options: Written warning or deferment of increment.
questions.explanation: PSR 100310 provides that lesser punishments such as written warning, deferment or withholding of increment, or reduction in rank may apply.

questions.question: Which **PSR** provision ensures an officer is given a fair hearing before any disciplinary action is taken?
questions.explanation: PSR 100303 guarantees an officer the right to a fair hearing consistent with Section 36 of the Constitution before disciplinary action.

questions.question: When an officer is suspended, what salary status applies?
options: Full pay until conclusion of the case.
questions.explanation: PSR 100306 states that suspension for a serious offence is normally without pay while the case is under investigation.

questions.question: If an officer under suspension is acquitted, what should happen?
options: Reinstatement with restoration of full salary.
Submission of a separate appeal before return.
questions.explanation: PSR 100306(b) requires reinstatement and payment of withheld emoluments when a suspended officer is acquitted.

questions.question: Under the PSR, what follows termination rather than dismissal?
options: Forfeiture of benefits that would otherwise remain payable.
Honourable dismissal with automatic promotion rights.
The same consequences as dismissal in every case.
Automatic application to criminal offences only.
questions.explanation: Termination under the PSR is not the same as dismissal; it is cessation of appointment otherwise than by resignation, dismissal, or retirement.

questions.question: Which disciplinary measure can be imposed for persistent lateness or negligence not amounting to serious misconduct?
questions.explanation: PSR 100310 lists written warning as appropriate for minor cases of negligence or inefficiency.

questions.question: An officer may be dismissed without further disciplinary proceedings if what event has occurred?
options: Conviction by a court of law for a relevant offence.
Receipt of three queries within a year.
questions.explanation: PSR 100312 allows dismissal without further proceedings where the officer has been convicted by a court for an offence involving dishonesty or moral turpitude.

questions.question: Under **PSR**, who approves dismissal for officers on **GL.07** and above
questions.explanation: PSR 100303 and 100304 designate the Federal Civil Service Commission as the approving authority for dismissal of senior officers.

questions.question: Which committee handles disciplinary cases involving officers on **GL.06** and below?
questions.explanation: PSR 100304 provides that the Junior Staff Committee (Disciplinary) handles disciplinary cases for officers on GL.06 and below.

questions.question: How many days must elapse before an officer absent without leave is assumed to have abandoned duty?
questions.explanation: PSR 100308 specifies that an officer absent for 10 consecutive working days without permission is deemed to have abandoned duty.

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule allows the use of video or electronic evidence in disciplinary proceedings?
questions.explanation: PSR 100315 introduces acceptance of electronic and video evidence in line with modern administrative justice standards.

questions.question: Which rule empowers the Head of Service to direct reinstatement after an officer is cleared on appeal?
questions.explanation: PSR 100316 gives the Head of Service power to direct reinstatement after successful appeal or exoneration.

questions.question: What should happen to an officer's record after dismissal?
options: Retention of the record with the dismissal clearly marked.
Forwarding of the record to the civil registry only.
Erasure of the record after five years.
questions.explanation: PSR 100311(c) requires the officer's record to be marked as dismissed and retained for administrative reference.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the an officer under interdiction who takes up private employment before conclusion of his case commits?
questions.explanation: PSR 100305(e) forbids taking up private employment while under interdiction; such act constitutes serious misconduct.

questions.question: Where a disciplinary decision is made in error or without due process, the **PSR** provides that?
options: The FCSC may review and reverse it.
The Head of Service must approve it.
questions.explanation: PSR 100316 permits the FCSC to review and reverse any disciplinary decision made in error or without due process.

questions.question: What is the responsibility of Permanent Secretaries after disciplinary proceedings have been concluded?
options: Forward copies of decisions to OHCSF and FCSC.
questions.explanation: PSR 100314 mandates submission of disciplinary outcomes to OHCSF and FCSC for record and oversight.

questions.question: Which **PSR** rule addresses the disciplinary process in parastatals and agencies?
questions.explanation: PSR 170305 aligns parastatal disciplinary procedure with Chapter 10 of PSR, requiring Board oversight and OHCSF notification.

questions.question: If a dismissed officer later wins an appeal, which right is restored under the PSR?
options: Restoration of all rights, emoluments, and benefits.
No restoration of benefits after dismissal.
questions.explanation: PSR 100316(b) provides that a successful appeal restores the officer's rights, emoluments, and benefits as if the dismissal had not occurred.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the a disciplinary case against a deceased officer should be?
questions.explanation: PSR 100309(c) directs that disciplinary proceedings terminate upon the death of the officer.

questions.question: If a disciplinary case leads to dismissal and the officer appeals unsuccessfully, can he be re-employed
options: Only with approval of the Federal Civil Service Commission.
questions.explanation: PSR 100316(d) permits re-employment of a dismissed officer only upon FCSC approval.

questions.question: An officer on trial in court must be placed on what status pending judgment
options: Suspension or interdiction depending on gravity.
questions.explanation: PSR 100305 and 100306 provide for interdiction or suspension pending court outcome depending on offence severity.

questions.question: If an officer under investigation absconds, what disciplinary action applies?
options: Salary stoppage with ongoing investigation only.
questions.explanation: PSR 100308 treats abscondment as desertion and provides for dismissal where the officer abandons duty while under investigation.

questions.question: What must accompany all disciplinary proceedings records forwarded to the Commission?
options: A full set of evidence, minutes and recommendation.
questions.explanation: PSR 100314 requires that disciplinary records forwarded to FCSC must include evidence, minutes and recommendations.

questions.question: When a disciplinary case involves financial loss to Government, how is the amount treated?
options: Recovery from the officer after proven culpability.
Equal sharing among officers in the unit.
Payment from the ministry's imprest account.
questions.explanation: PSR 100307(b) directs recovery of financial loss from an officer whose culpability has been established through disciplinary proceedings.

questions.question: Under **PSR**, which penalty results in forfeiture of all pension and gratuity rights
questions.explanation: PSR 100311(c) explicitly states dismissal results in forfeiture of all claims to terminal benefits.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the an officer who falsifies his age at entry into service commits?
questions.explanation: PSR 100201(f) classifies falsification of records, including age, as serious misconduct punishable by dismissal.

questions.question: What is the purpose of maintaining disciplinary registers in Ministries?
options: To record staff disciplinary outcomes for reference and audit.
questions.explanation: PSR 100314 requires Ministries to maintain disciplinary registers for record, monitoring and audit purposes.

questions.question: Under **PSR**, which of the following may not appeal a disciplinary decision
questions.explanation: PSR 100316(a) allows appeals only by living officers; proceedings terminate upon death of the officer.

questions.question: Under **PSR** 2021, what is the maximum timeline within which the Federal Civil Service Commission should conclude disciplinary cases referred to it
questions.explanation: PSR 100309(b) provides that FCSC should ensure disciplinary cases are concluded within six months from commencement.

questions.question: Which action best demonstrates sound governance in discipline and misconduct administration?
options: Approved procedure supported by complete records.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Sound governance in disciplinary administration depends on following approved procedure and keeping complete records that can be reviewed later.

questions.question: For effective discipline & misconduct, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure discipline & misconduct compliance
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best supports risk management in discipline and misconduct cases?
options: Early identification of control gaps with prompt escalation of material exceptions.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Risk management improves when control gaps are identified early and material exceptions are escalated before they damage the disciplinary process.

questions.question: When handling discipline & misconduct, which choice reflects proper appointment governance standards?
options: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain disciplinary process in Discipline & Misconduct?
options: Observe due process, fair hearing, and documented decisions.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Observe due process, fair hearing, and documented decisions. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Discipline & Misconduct, which action best demonstrates leave administration
options: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which approach best preserves promotion standards in discipline and misconduct administration?
options: Eligibility confirmation before recommendation for advancement.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Promotion standards are preserved when eligibility is checked before advancement is recommended, rather than being left to inconsistency or convenience.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on circular compliance within Discipline & Misconduct?
options: Align practice with current circular directives and PSR rules.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Align practice with current circular directives and psr rules. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct.

questions.question: Which practice best reflects proper documented procedure in discipline and misconduct matters?
options: Approved steps supported by complete records.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Proper documented procedure means following the approved steps and keeping a complete record so the matter can be reviewed at any stage.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain compliance assurance in Discipline & Misconduct?
options: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best protects fairness in a disciplinary inquiry under the PSR?
options: Opportunity for officer response before decision.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Fairness in a disciplinary inquiry requires giving the officer an opportunity to respond before a decision is taken, which reflects fair hearing under the PSR.

questions.question: For effective discipline & misconduct, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure service integrity
options: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on risk control within discipline and misconduct?
options: Early risk identification with documented mitigation.
Inconsistent rule application across similar cases.
questions.explanation: Early risk identification with documented mitigation is the best answer because it reduces exposure and leaves an audit trail.

questions.question: When handling discipline & misconduct, which choice reflects proper decision transparency standards?
options: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain operational discipline in discipline and misconduct administration?
options: Approved workflow with verification of outputs before closure.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
questions.explanation: Operational discipline is sustained when officers follow the approved workflow and verify outputs before closing the matter.

questions.question: In the context of Discipline & Misconduct, which action best demonstrates citizen-focused service
options: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which approach best preserves record management in discipline and misconduct administration?
options: Accurate file maintenance with status updates at each control point.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Record management in disciplinary matters depends on accurate file maintenance and status updates at each control point.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on performance standards within Discipline & Misconduct?
options: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best reflects proper governance standards in discipline and misconduct matters?
options: Approved procedure supported by complete records.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Proper governance standards in disciplinary matters require approved procedure and complete records so accountability and fairness can be demonstrated later.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain discipline & misconduct compliance in Discipline & Misconduct?
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Discipline & Misconduct, which action best demonstrates discipline & misconduct risk management
options: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: For effective discipline & misconduct, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure appointment governance
options: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Apply merit-based criteria and authorized approval channels. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best aligns with due process in discipline and misconduct administration?
options: Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
questions.explanation: Due process requires fair hearing and documented decisions so the outcome remains procedurally sound and reviewable.

questions.question: When handling discipline & misconduct, which choice reflects proper leave administration standards?
options: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Apply leave rules consistently and update personnel records. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in discipline & misconduct Reference: Discipline & Misconduct - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain promotion standards in discipline and misconduct administration?
options: Eligibility confirmation before recommendation for advancement.
Bypassing of review and approval checkpoints.
Preference for convenience over compliance requirements.
questions.explanation: Promotion standards are sustained when eligibility is confirmed before advancement is recommended, keeping the process fair and rule-based.

questions.question: What does "Interdiction" mean under the PSR?
options: Temporary removal from normal duties on half salary during dismissal proceedings.
questions.explanation: Under Rule 010105, interdiction is the temporary removal of an officer from normal duties while disciplinary proceedings for dismissal are being undertaken or are about to be undertaken, with placement on half salary pending determination.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines the term "the contravention of Rule 020107 regarding the date of birth regarded as" according to official standards?
questions.explanation: Rule 020107 treats the contravention as serious misconduct because false or improper birth-date declaration undermines trust and service records.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines the term "the contravention of Rule 020211 (Secret Societies) regarded as" according to official standards?
questions.explanation: Rule 020211 treats membership in secret societies as serious misconduct because it conflicts with public-service integrity and accountability.

questions.question: What disciplinary action may be taken for contravening Rule 020211?
questions.explanation: The rule provides dismissal from the service as the disciplinary action for contravening Rule 020211.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the the 'whistleblowing' policy encourages officers to report?
options: Acts of misconduct, corruption, or illegality
questions.explanation: The whistleblowing policy encourages officers to report misconduct, corruption, and illegality so that wrongdoing can be checked early.

questions.question: Ultimate penalty for serious misconduct resulting in forfeiture of benefits?
questions.explanation: Dismissal is the ultimate penalty for serious misconduct and may lead to forfeiture of benefits under the PSR.

questions.question: Maximum penalty for breach of the Code of Conduct?
questions.explanation: The maximum penalty for a breach of the Code of Conduct is dismissal, imprisonment, and disqualification, depending on the offense and the legal process.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the corruption, fraud, and criminal convictions are categorized as?
questions.explanation: Corruption, fraud, and criminal convictions are classified as serious misconduct because they involve grave breaches of public trust.

questions.question: When may Government refuse a resignation?
options: Pending disciplinary proceedings against the officer.
Occupancy of a sensitive position alone.
questions.explanation: Government may refuse a resignation when disciplinary proceedings are pending against the officer, because the case must first be resolved under the applicable rules.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the term "termination of appointment" as used in the Public Service Rules?
options: Cessation of appointment by Government otherwise than by resignation, dismissal or retirement.
questions.explanation: Termination of appointment means cessation of appointment by government otherwise than by resignation, dismissal, or retirement.

questions.question: What does dismissal mean under the PSR?
options: Termination of appointment as a disciplinary measure for serious misconduct.
questions.explanation: Under the PSR, dismissal means termination of appointment imposed as a disciplinary punishment for serious misconduct, not a voluntary or temporary separation.

questions.question: What salary position applies during interdiction under the PSR?
questions.explanation: Interdiction places the officer on half salary while the disciplinary process is pending, which distinguishes it from full-duty service and from suspension.

questions.question: What condition justifies interdiction?
options: Mere suspicion without a prima facie case.
Pending dismissal proceedings with a prima facie case.
Commission of a minor offence only.
questions.explanation: Interdiction is justified where dismissal proceedings are being undertaken or are about to be undertaken and a prima facie case exists.

questions.question: What salary position applies during suspension under the PSR?
questions.explanation: Suspension carries no salary entitlement under the PSR, which is why it is treated more severely than interdiction.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recognized condition for suspension?
options: Where a prima facie case of a serious nature has been established.
questions.explanation: Suspension applies where a prima facie case of a serious nature has been established.

questions.question: What action applies if an officer's date of birth recorded on appointment is changed without due authority?
options: Treatment as an act of serious misconduct.
questions.explanation: Rule 020108 states that an unauthorized change of date of birth recorded on appointment is treated as an act of serious misconduct and may lead to dismissal.

questions.question: What consequence follows if a public officer is found to be a member of a secret society?
questions.explanation: Rule 020212 states that contravention of Rule 020211 on membership of a secret society must be regarded as an act of serious misconduct, which may include dismissal from the service. The consequence being tested is therefore serious misconduct leading to dismissal.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines the term "the primary role of the Code of Conduct Bureau in relation to public officers" according to official standards?
options: To receive declarations of assets and liabilities from public officers.
To set salary structures for the Civil Service.
questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct Bureau receives written declarations of assets and liabilities from public officers, including declarations covering relevant family interests where required by law.

questions.question: The workflow for terminating a contract appointment?
options: The contract is terminated at the discretion of the Permanent Secretary.
A letter of termination is issued directly by the Ministry.
A simple verbal notification is sufficient.3.
A recommendation from the Federal Civil Service Commission is demand.
questions.explanation: Correct option: D (A recommendation from the Federal Civil Service Commission is demand.). Rule 020407 states that an appointment on contract may be terminated by the government in accordance with the terms specified, and a recommendation is sent to the Federal Civil Service Commission35.

questions.question: Which option correctly addresses: An officer may be dismissed from service for what reason?
questions.explanation: Correct option: B (Gross misconduct). Rule 020903 defines "termination" but does not explicitly mention "dismissal" in the provided text. However, Rule 100407 defines dismissal and its effects. This question is a knowledge gap in the provided text. Rule 020212 mentions that an act of serious misconduct may include dismissal from the service. Rule 100401 defines gross misconduct. Therefore, the most appropriate answer is "Gross misconduct."

questions.question: The consequence for an officer who receives information about a colleague's resignation but fails to promptly inform the Accounts Department?
options: They will be transferred to another department.
They will be liable to surcharge and discipline for negligence.
They will receive a formal warning.
They will have their own salary delayed.
questions.explanation: Correct option: B (They will be liable to surcharge and discipline for negligence.). According to Financial Regulation 1508, an officer who fails to promptly pass on information to the Director, Finance and Accounts Department is liable to surcharge and discipline for negligence and dereliction of duty.

questions.question: Choose the best answer for: What emerging issues are reflected in the 2021 edition of the Public Service Rules?
options: Performance Management System, Talent Sourcing, Volunteerism, Virtual Meetings/Engagements.
questions.explanation: Correct option: B (Performance Management System, Talent Sourcing, Volunteerism, Virtual Meetings/Engagements.).
